text: A negative training-stress balance (acute workload exceeding chronic workload) is associated with increased injury risk in the following week, with relative risk of 2.2 for internal workload and 2.1 for external workload.
Athletes whose acute internal workload exceeds 200% of their chronic workload have a 4.5 times greater injury risk compared to those maintaining a 50-99% training-stress balance.
Athletes whose acute external workload exceeds 200% of their chronic workload have a 3.3 times greater injury risk compared to those maintaining a 50-99% training-stress balance.
Chronic workload should be calculated as a 4-week rolling average, and acute workload as the most recent 1-week total, to assess training-stress balance and injury risk.
Both internal workload (session RPE × duration) and external workload measures show similar associations with injury risk when acute workload spikes relative to chronic workload.
Maintaining an acute-to-chronic workload ratio between 50% and 99% is associated with the lowest injury risk and can serve as a safe training zone.
